coredata.dc:title: Visual monitoring of trace water in organic solvents based on ecofriendly b/r-CDs ratiometric fluorescence test paper
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xiqing
Zhou, Zhiping
Wang, Tao
Deng, Pengyin
Yan, Yongsheng
coredata.dc:description: Developing a green, non-toxic and easy to synthesize of fluorescence probe for fast and visual detecting trace water in various organic solvents was an important task. Here, a novel dual-emission fluorescence probe (b/r-CDs) was designed based on the red CDs (r-CDs) and blue CDs (b-CDs) to detect the trace water and enhance the visualization for naked-eye observation in different organic solvents. Among, the red fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) was found to have the capability to monitor trace amounts of water, which synthesized with green tea by facile ultrasonic method. Further, Such a dual-emission probe could fast monitor trace water in various organic solvents with high stability and fast response. Importantly, a synergistic mechanism of the dynamic process (b-CDs) and static quenching (r-CDs) was proved for the study of water detection. Moreover, the test paper was made for detecting trace water in different organic solvents, achieving convenient and effective detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emission b/r-CDs
Synergistic process
Visualization
Test strips
Trace water

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detecting trace water in the organic solvent
Preparation of dual-emission b/r-CDs test paper for detecting water in organic environments

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of b-CDs (a) and r-CDs (b) with different excitation wavelengths, TEM images of b-CDs (c) and r-CDs (d).

Fluorescence spectra (a) and the corresponding linear relationship (b) of dual-emission b/r-CDs in EtOH with an increase of the amount of water, the stability of dual-emission b/r-CDs in EtOH (c), the optimum response time for dual-emission b/r-CDs to 1.5% water in EtOH (d), fluorescence spectra (e) and the corresponding linear relationship (f) of r-CDs in EtOH with an increase of the amount of water.
UV–vis spectra of b-CDs, r-CDs and dual-emission b/r-CDs in EtOH with the presence or absence of water.
Time-resolved fluorescence spectra of r-CDs in EtOH (a) and EtOH-water solution (b), b-CDs in EtOH (c) and EtOH-water solution (d).
The possible fluorescence mechanism of r-CDs in organic solvents with water.

Photographs of dual-emission b/r-CDs in EtOH with different amount water under UV lamp (a), photos of test paper dripped with different water amount in EtOH under ambient light (b) and UV light (c), CIE chromaticity diagram of dual-emission b/r-CDs with various addition amount of water (d).
Linear range and detection limit in different organic solvents.

head.ce:title: Visual monitoring of trace water in organic solvents based on ecofriendly b/r-CDs ratiometric fluorescence test paper

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, Jiangsu University, 212013, Zhenjiang, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu Province, 86-212013, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing a green, non-toxic and easy to synthesize of fluorescence probe for fast and visual detecting trace water in various organic solvents was an important task. Here, a novel dual-emission fluorescence probe (b/r-CDs) was designed based on the red CDs (r-CDs) and blue CDs (b-CDs) to detect the trace water and enhance the visualization for naked-eye observation in different organic solvents. Among, the red fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) was found to have the capability to monitor trace amounts of water, which synthesized with green tea by facile ultrasonic method. Further, Such a dual-emission probe could fast monitor trace water in various organic solvents with high stability and fast response. Importantly, a synergistic mechanism of the dynamic process (b-CDs) and static quenching (r-CDs) was proved for the study of water detection. Moreover, the test paper was made for detecting trace water in different organic solvents, achieving convenient and effective detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Zero-dimensional (0D) carbon dots (CDs) have received much attention due to their unique optical, chemical and physical features [1–4]. CDs can always be modified with many surface functionalities, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and amide, which increased CDs solubility, stability and functionalization in a biological environment [5,6]. Coupled with their simple synthetic process, low toxic, environmental-friendly and low cost, CDs have attracted enough attentions in extensive scope in antibacterial [7–9], photovoltaic devices [10], catalysis [11,12] and chemical sensing [13,14]. Particularly, CDs emerged as luminescent materials for chemical sensing. For example, A tricolor emission multiple-cores@shell CDs were synthesized and used as cell imaging marker [15]. Lu et al. [16] synthesized dual-functional CDs for bioimaging and antitumor therapy. Although CDs have great applications in biosensing and fluorescence images due to its photoluminescence properties, only a few reports described that the CDs could as a fluorescent probe to sensing the trance water of organic solvent.

Sensitively and rapidly sensing drop water in many organic environments and eliminating the chemical and industrial production process was of great importance [17–20]. Considering the limitation of the most popular method of Karl-Fisher was noxious chemical, sensitive to interference and incompetence of monitoring in time, thus, several optical methods have developed for detecting trace water because of its easy fabrication, sensitivity and selectively. For example, Zhou et al. [21] studied versatile lead-free luminescent perovskite bulk materials for the application in water detection. Deng et al. [22] used poly(3-aminobenzoic acid) as the sensor to assay the trace mount water in organic solvents by the fluorescence method. Manolis et al. [23] developed a turn-on luminescence sensor of Mg2+ metal-organic framework (MOF) for detecting the water content. Unfortunately, most of the fluorescence probe was based on single-emission detection could be interfered by probe concentration, environmental conditions and instrument sensitivity. In contrast, the dual-emission fluorescence probes showed two different emission peaks under a single excitation state, which could improve the accuracy due to the inherent rectification for some capriciousness [24,25]. As a result, to design a new dual-emission fluorescence probe for detecting water in organic environments was still an urgent challenge.

So far, several dual-emission luminescent probes have been developed for trace water detection. In addition, a series of mechanisms was also explored, including photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [26–28], aggregation-induced emission (AIE) [29], intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) [30,31], Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [32,33] and so on, which was benefit to further improve the detection sensitivity. For example, Song et al. [34] prepared a dual-emission water detector by b-CDs and red fluorescence Cu nanoclusters and based on the AIE properties of Cu nanoclusters to sense water. Wang et al. [35] found a dual-emission fluorescence sensor to respond to water content because of strong ICT effect based on coumarin conjugate. Yang et al. [36] used the dansyl and rhodamine mimic to construct a dual-emission fluorescence probe, and the probe showed a fast monitor trace water in organic environments in view of the FRET platform. Li et al. [37] reported a sensor Tb97.11Eu2.89-L1 to response to trace water in organic environments and variable emissive light colors visible to naked eyes. Those dual-emission fluorescence probes have certain conquer such as single emission, accuracy and visualization. Nevertheless, these dual-emission probes were complex in synthesis, difficult to design, contains rare earth elements and toxicity.

Herein, a dual-emission of blue-red CDs (b/r-CDs) fluorescence probe was designed to detect trace water with the lower background interference and stronger visualization for the first time to report. Among, natural red fluorescence CDs (r-CDs) as the recognition signal to monitor the trace water in different organic environments at first, was obtained by ultrasonic treatment of green tea powder. The installation was obtained by dispersing blue fluorescence CDs (b-CDs) and r-CDs in the organic solvent to show two emissions (436 nm for b-CDs and 674 nm for r-CDs) under an optimum single excitation wavelength. The dual-emission b/r-CDs only showed strong red fluorescence in a different anhydrous organic solvent at the beginning. With the gradual addition of water, the blue fluorescence of b-CDs was increased and the red fluorescence of r-CDs was quenched simultaneously. Additionally, the static and dynamic coordination process of dual-emission b/r-CDs was demonstrated for the study of water detection. Therefore, the test strip was prepared for quick and easy water detection in organic solvents due to the color of dual-emission probes could change obviously based on the static and dynamic coordination process, which can be easily observed by naked eyes.
The detailed content was added in the support information.

Dual-emission b/r-CDs fluorescence probe was facilely synthesized by mixing blue and r-CDs. As we know, the dual-emission fluorescence probes usually showed two different emission peaks under a single excitation state[38,39]. As Fig. 1 a showed the better fluorescence emission intensity of b-CDs was about at 340–380 nm excitation. While the r-CDs showed better fluorescence emission peaks at 380–410 nm excitation (Fig. 1b). According to Fig. 1, the 380 nm was selected as the optimum excitation for the dual-emission b/r-CDs in the whole experiments.

To understanding the origin size effect on the fluorescence property of CDs, the morphologies of the red and b-CDs were observed by TEM. As the TEM images showed (Fig. 1c and d), the b-CDs and r-CDs were excellent dispersion and size uniform. The particle sizes of b-CDs and r-CDs were about 1.8 nm and 3.5 nm, respectively.

Moreover, the UV–vis absorption spectra of b-CDs and r-CDs showed similar characteristics. Fig. S1 exhibited a shift toward longer wavelength for r-CDs compared to b-CDs, providing a more evident evidence that the absorption bands of CDs were linked to the size. Interestingly, a absorb peak was found at 667 nm for the r-CDs, which was well matched with the UV–vis spectra of reported chlorophyll [40,41]. Thus, the red fluorescence was maybe attributed to the structure of chlorophyll, which was constructed by the porphyrin Mg compound (Fig. S2). Furthermore, the comparison of FT-IR spectra of tea leaves and r-CDs was further demonstrated the r-CDs has the chemical structure of chlorophyll (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting trace water in the organic solvent
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence property of dual-emission b/r-CDs for detecting trace water in organic solvents was determined at room temperature, which was the primary performance for monitoring trace water in the organic environments. The EtOH was used as a typical representative for detailed discussing. With the gradual addition of water, it clearly observed the red fluorescence (r-CDs) was quenched gradually, while the blue fluorescence (b-CDs) was enhanced (Fig. 2 a) in EtOH. The decreased red fluorescence of r-CDs may be attributed to the formation of Mg porphyrin-water complex, the details and more evident were discussed in the following. The increased blue fluorescence was attributed to the solvent effect. It should be clear that the blue fluorescence was always existed in the dual-emission b/r-CDs, which can be proved by the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2a). The reason was attributed to the solvent effect, indicating b-CDs has greater dispersion in water. Such double fluorescence change was more conducive to visual observation. Moreover, the corresponding linear relationship was illustrated in Fig. 2b. The linear relationship of dual-emission b/r-CDs with two emission wavelengths (I674/I436) and water content change from 0.0% to 5.0%. The linear equation could be calculated as log[(I674/436)0/(I674/436)] = 19.98262[c]-0.00856, where R2 = 0.9950. The results demonstrated that the dual-emission fluorescence probe can be applied to detect trace water in EtOH and the calculated minimum detection volume was 0.07% in EtOH.

Considering the practical application, stability and response time were analyzed. As Fig. 2c showed the dual-emission b/r-CDs keep the constant fluorescence intensity within 60 min, suggesting the dual-emission sensor has the potential for applications. Equally important was that the response time of the fluorescence was 30 s (Fig. 2d). Wonderful, the dual-emission b/r-CDs could achieve fast and trace water detection in EtOH. As a comparison, the linear relationship of single r-CDs was also presented (Fig. 2e and f). It was worth referring that the single r-CDs showed a better linear relationship with the water concentration from 0.0% to 5.0%, and the linear regression formula was log[(I674)0/I674] = 14.08466[c]+0.00174, where R2 = 0.9974. The minimum detection volume was 0.11% for detecting trace water in EtOH. The dual-emission b/r-CDs displayed lower detection volume and greater visual observation to water in EtOH.

As shown in Figs. S4–9, the ability of dual-emission b/r-CDs for monitoring trace water in different organic environments was also explored, including toluene, acetone, cyclohexane, MeOH, acetonitrile and n-hexanol. As expected, a similar phenomenon was observed in each organic solvent, respectively. With the gradual addition of water, the red fluorescence intensities of r-CDs were decreased while the blue fluorescence intensities of b-CDs were increases for each organic solvent (Figs. S4c, 5c, 6c, 7c, 8c, 9c). Importantly, the dual-emission fluorescence probe was still kept perfect stability and fast response speed (within 1.0 min) in different organic solvents (Figs. S4–9). Therefore, the dual-emission has the ability to sense the trace water in various organic solvents. As a comparison, the ability to detect trace water of single r-CDs was determined at the same conditions. The detection results demonstrated the linear relationship of dual-emission b/r-CDs was not enhanced obviously than single r-CDs, but the naked eye visualization will be greater. The stability, response time, fluorescence emission spectra, and corresponding linear relationship were illustrated in Figs. S4–9. The results demonstrated that the dual-emission b/r-CDs could use to detect trace water in various organic solvents. Meanwhile, the comparison of the linear range and detection limit of trace water detection in different organic solvents of dual-emission b/r-CDs and single r-CDs were presented in Table 1 . Although the minimum detection volume was not improved obviously in some organic solvents, the dual-emission probe has greater visual observation for naked-eye observation.

To study the sensing mechanism was an important means to develop, improve and optimize the application. As all known that inner filter effect (IFE) [42,43], dynamic quenching [44,45] and static quenching [46,47] were the main fluorescence quenching mechanism. Among, IFE referred to a phenomenon in which the absorption spectrum of the target coincided with the excitation or emission spectra of the probe. Here, the water as the target was neither shielded the excitation nor absorb the emission light, suggesting the IFE was ruled out for this detection system. Additionally, the UV–vis absorption spectra for CDs (including b/r-CDs, b -CDs and r-CDs) and CDs-water was further tested in EtOH (Fig. 3 ). Apparently, the absorption peak was shifted for r-CDs-water, suggesting the formation of r-CDs-water complex. However, the absorption spectra of b-CDs were consistent in the presence or absence of water, suggesting no complex was formed between b-CDs and water. In the system of dual-emission b/r-CDs-water, the two phenomena appeared simultaneously. Based on the above results, a synchronization process of the dynamic and static process was happened in the dual-emission b/r-CDs for b-CDs and r-CDs.

To further confirmed the inference, the fluorescence lifetime (τ) was tested in EtOH. It knows that the fluorescence lifetime was constant for static quenching, while the lifetime would change in the absence and presence of target for the dynamic process[48,49]. As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, the data were well fitted with the mono-exponential equation for the r-CDs in EtOH with absence or presence of water. The result of fluorescence lifetime of r-CDs was about 53.529 ns (without water) and 54.322 ns (with water) in EtOH solvents, which exact confirmed the statics quenching mechanism for the water detection of r-CDs. For b-CDs, the data were well fitted with three exponential equation (Fig. 4c and d) in EtOH and the average fluorescence lifetime was calculated by the formula of Σbiτi products. The three fluorescence decay components of b-CDs were τ1 (4.187 ns), τ2 (20.836 ns) and τ3 (105.233 ns), and the average τ was about 38.324 ns. After the addition of water, the average τ was increased, τ1 (3.721 ns), τ2 (20.072 ns) and τ3 (115.143 ns), and the average τ were about 56.880 ns. The increased was further exact the dynamic process of b-CDs in the absence or presence of water, suggesting the extended radiation process due to the solvent effect of b-CDs in the water.

The theoretical calculation method was an effective approach to deeper learn the chemical process. Based on the above analysis, the red fluorescence was mainly from the structure of porphyrinic Mg [24]. Among the dual-emission fluorescence probe, the r-CDs as recognition signal was detected with water, while the b-CDs enhanced visualization as a reference signal. Therefore, the quenching process of r-CDs was investigated via previous researches [50,51]. Tatiana group analyzed various types of interactions (π-π stacking, coordinative, hydrogen bonding) chlorophyllide with water was explored by various methods, such as symmetry-adapted perturbation theory (SAPT), density-functional theory (DFT), functional-group SAPT (F-SAPT) and time-dependent DFT [52]. The studies demonstrated the water tend to inrush the structure of porphyrinic Mg to form a complex via the bond of Mg-O. Additional, the simulated absorb spectra proved the complexation has an effect on absorb resulted in the shift of the peaks’ position, which was consistent with our experimental results. Therefore, the r-CDs was quenched due to the formation of complexation of water and a possible detection process was proposed in Fig. 5 .

Based on the above analysis, the chlorophyll structure was found in the fluoresces materials of r-CDs. Combining the result of UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetime, the static quenching mechanism was proposed. In addition, the Tatiana group's research was strongly demonstrated that the quenching process was the formation of chlorophyll-water complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of dual-emission b/r-CDs test paper for detecting water in organic environments
ce:section.ce:para: To spread the water monitoring by such dual-emission b/r-CDs in different organic solvents, the test strips were used as the easy and fast tool, which made by drenching filter in EtOH of dual-emission b/r-CDs and dried naturally. The test strips were used to sensing the trace water in EtOH, MeOH, toluene, acetone, cyclohexane, acetonitrile and n-hexanol solutions containing various amount of water, respectively. It clearly observed that the fluorescence color of paper strips was turned from red to blue gradually in different organic solvents under UV lamp, with the gradually adding water (Fig. 6c, S10-15c). As expected, the test strips did not show obvious color change under natural light (Fig. 6 b, Fig. S10-15b). In addition, the CIE (Commission Internationale d’Eclairage 1931) diagram used to observe the fluorescence color of the dual-emission probes with the gradually changed amount of water in different organic solvents (Fig. 6d, Fig. S10-15d). It showed that the distinctive color was turned from red to blue with the increased water in the organic solvents. This CIE result were consistent with the photographs under a UV lamp (Fig. 6a, Fig. S10-15a). Such test strips could use as potential monitoring tools for water detection with a swift and well-marked fluorescence color change for naked eyes observation.

In summary, a dual-emission of blue-red CDs (b/r-CDs) fluorescence probe was designed to detect trace water. Among, the red fluorescence CDs as the monitor was fist found has the excellent ability to detect trace water in different organic solvents, which obtained by ultrasound the green tea powder in EtOH solvent. Experimental and theoretical studies revealed that an r-CDs-water complex was formed resulted in fluorescence quenching. The r-CDs exhibited better stability, fast response time and low detection limit in different organic solvents. Moreover, dual-emission b/r-CDs was prepared with two emission bands at 436 nm (b-CDs) and 674 nm (r-CDs) under a single excitation wavelength. Such dual-emission exhibited good trace water response property at different organic solvents. Interestingly, a synergistic mechanism of the dynamic process (b-CDs) and static quenching (r-CDs) was further enhanced the visual effects. The fluorescence intensity of r-CDs was decreased with the gradual addition of water, while the fluorescence intensity of b-CDs was increased. Importantly, the dual-emission b/r-CDs was used to prepare a test paper for convenient and visual detecting trace water in different organic solvents. Therefore, a new discovery was provided a new sight for r-CDs and a nontoxic fluorescence probe for trace water detection.

Xiqing Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Zhiping Zhou: Writing - review & editing. Tao Wang: Data curation. Pengyin Deng: Data curation. Yongsheng Yan: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from the National Science Foundation of China (21908080), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Provincial (BK20180884).
